original_text: The stock market sees a 20% drop in the S&P 500 over six months, triggering fears of a recession.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The stock market sees a 55% crash in the S&P 500 over six months, plunging the economy into a severe recession with mass layoffs, widespread bank failures, and a systemic financial crisis.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The stock market sees a 40% plunge in the S&P 500 over six months, triggering a confirmed recession with significant job losses and a sharp contraction in consumer spending.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The stock market sees a 30% decline in the S&P 500 over six months, pushing the economy to the brink of recession with rising unemployment and falling business confidence.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The stock market sees a 20% drop in the S&P 500 over six months, triggering fears of a recession.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The stock market sees a 12% pullback in the S&P 500 over six months, raising concerns about an economic slowdown but with most indicators remaining stable.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The stock market sees a 7% dip in the S&P 500 over six months, causing brief investor unease but with the broader economy continuing to show resilient growth.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The stock market sees a 3% fluctuation in the S&P 500 over six months, reflecting routine market volatility with no meaningful impact on economic growth or employment.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier describes the same core scenario—an S&P 500 movement over six months—but varies the magnitude of the drop and the severity of its economic consequences. The percentage decline decreases monotonically from 55% to 3%, and the described economic fallout scales accordingly, from systemic financial crisis to routine volatility with no real impact.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a 3% routine fluctuation with no economic harm is clearly a better outcome than any significant market crash or recession. A rational agent prefers minimal economic disruption, job security, and financial stability.
